function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Segment Adjusted EBITDA is the filing’s primary non-GAAP measure. Relative to segment operating income, identify each add-back and explain why understanding construction of the metric matters before comparing segment economics.
I'll start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and then retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation details.

1. First, parse the SpaceX S-1 filing to access its content
2. Then retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation information from the filing

Let me start by parsing the filing and then retrieving the relevant section.

steps.message: Now let me retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table and definition from the filing.
steps.reasoning_content: Good, the filing is parsed. Now let me retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation details.

arguments.prompt: What is the definition of Segment Adjusted EBITDA and what are the add-backs or adjustments used to reconcile from segment operating income to Segment Adjusted EBITDA? Look for the reconciliation table and the description of each adjustment line item. {{spacex_s1}}

Find the MD&A section that discusses Segment Adjusted EBITDA as a non-GAAP measure, including any tables showing the reconciliation from segment operating income (loss) to Segment Adjusted EBITDA for each segment (Launch Services and Satellite Services). List each line item add-back. {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the financial data you provided, here is the definition and reconciliation for **Segment Adjusted EBITDA**.

### Definition
**Segment Adjusted EBITDA** is a non-GAAP financial measure defined as a segment's income (loss) from operations, with the following items added back (excluded):
*   Depreciation and amortization
*   Share-based compensation
*   Restructuring charges
*   Impairment

This measure is used by management to assess segment financial performance, as it removes non-cash and non-recurring charges that do not directly reflect ongoing operational results.

### Reconciliation: Segment Income (Loss) from Operations to Segment Adjusted EBITDA
The reconciliation consistently follows this format, as shown in the tables for the three months ended March 31, 2025/2026 and the years ended December 31, 2023/2024/2025:

**Starting Point:** Segment Income (Loss) from Operations
**Add-backs (Adjustments):**
1.  **Depreciation and amortization:** Non-cash expense representing the allocation of the cost of tangible and intangible assets over their useful lives.
2.  **Share-based compensation:** Non-cash expense related to stock-based awards granted to employees.
3.  **Restructuring charges:** Costs associated with reorganizing and restructuring operations, such as severance or facility closure costs.
4.  **Impairment:** Non-cash write-down of the value of long-lived assets when their recoverable amount is less than their carrying amount.

**Result:** Segment Adjusted EBITDA

### Example from Data (Three Months Ended March 31, 2025)
This table excerpt demonstrates the reconciliation for all reportable segments:

| (in millions) | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $(70) | $1,033 | $(936) | $27 |
| **Add:** | | | | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 162 | 510 | 771 | 1,443 |
| Share-based compensation | 108 | 75 | 49 | 232 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | 4 | 4 |
| Impairment | 24 | — | — | 24 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$224** | **$1,618** | **$(112)** | **$1,730** |

This same structure—adding back the same four line items—is consistently applied across all the reconciliation tables provided in your text, confirming the definition and methodology for calculating Segment Adjusted EBITDA.

Based on the provided document excerpts, here is the requested information from the Management's Discussion and Analysis (MD&A) section regarding Segment Adjusted EBITDA.

### 1. Definition and Role as a Non-GAAP Measure

The MD&A section defines **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** as a non-GAAP financial measure. It is a key performance measure used by management to assess the company's financial performance and for internal planning and forecasting. Management considers it a meaningful measure for investors to evaluate operating performance and compare results across periods by excluding the impact of certain non-cash and non-operating items.

It is explicitly stated that this measure **should not be considered in isolation or as a substitute for GAAP measures** like net income (loss) or income (loss) from operations, and should be reviewed alongside the corresponding GAAP measures and reconciliations.

### 2. Reconciliation from Segment Operating Income (Loss) to Segment Adjusted EBITDA

Segment Adjusted EBITDA is calculated by starting with the GAAP measure **Segment Income (Loss) from Operations** and adding back the following line items:

*   **Depreciation and amortization**
*   **Share-based compensation**
*   **Restructuring charges**
*   **Impairment**

### 3. Reconciliation Tables

The document provides detailed reconciliation tables for each reported period. Below is a consolidated view of the add-backs for the **three months ended March 31, 2026**, as a representative example.

**Three Months Ended March 31, 2026**
*(in millions)*

| Line Item Add-back | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total Reportable Segments |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $(662) | $1,188 | $(2,469) | $(1,943) |
| **Add:** | | | | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 166 | 783 | 1,493 | 2,442 |
| Share-based compensation | 145 | 116 | 378 | 639 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | (11) | (11) |
| Impairment | — | — | — | — |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$(351)** | **$2,087** | **$(609)** | **$1,127** |

The document contains similar reconciliation tables for other periods, including:
*   Three Months Ended March 31, 2025
*   Years Ended December 31, 2025, 2024, and 2023

For each segment and period, the process is identical: **Segment Adjusted EBITDA = Segment Income (Loss) from Operations + Depreciation and amortization + Share-based compensation + Restructuring charges + Impairment**.

results.content:
Based on the provided text from the filing, here are the answers to your questions:

### 1. Reportable Segments
The company has **three reportable segments**:
1.  **Space**
2.  **Connectivity**
3.  **AI**

These segments are defined in the Preamble section of the filing.

### 2. Management Discussion on Limitations, Use, and Potential Distortions

**Why Management Uses Adjusted EBITDA & Segment Adjusted EBITDA:**
*   Management considers Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA to be **"key performance measures"** used to assess financial performance and for **internal planning and forecasting**.
*   They believe these non-GAAP measures provide **"useful supplemental information"** and a **"meaningful view of our financial condition and results of operations across periods"** by removing items that do not directly reflect ongoing operating performance (like depreciation, share-based compensation, impairment, and restructuring charges).
*   They also consider them meaningful measures for **investors to evaluate operating performance and compare financial results**.

**Management Discussion of Limitations:**
The filing explicitly acknowledges the limitations of these non-GAAP measures:
*   **Comparability Issue:** "Other companies in our industry may calculate Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA differently than we do or may not calculate them at all, **limiting their usefulness as comparative measures**."
*   **Not a Substitute:** "Because of these limitations, Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA **should not be considered in isolation or as a substitute for** net income (loss), income (loss) from operations, or any other measure calculated in accordance with GAAP."
*   **Should be Used with GAAP:** These measures should be considered **together with GAAP financial measures and the reconciliations** to the most directly comparable GAAP measures.

**How Share-Based Compensation and Other Add-Backs May Distort Comparisons:**
While the filing does not contain a specific *management discussion* on the *distortion risk*, the mechanics of the add-backs create inherent comparability challenges:
*   **Distortion Between Segments:** Share-based compensation (SBC) and other add-backs are allocated unevenly across the segments. For example, in Q1 2026, the AI segment had $378 million in SBC added back, compared to $145 million for Space. This means the Segment Adjusted EBITDA for a segment heavily investing in stock-based compensation will be artificially boosted relative to one that is not, making direct segment-to-segment comparisons misleading without understanding the underlying GAAP expense allocations.
*   **Distortion Between Periods:** The amounts for SBC, restructuring, and impairments can fluctuate significantly year-over-year. For instance, total SBC increased from $784 million in 2024 to $1,947 million in 2025. This volatility means that Adjusted EBITDA trends may not accurately reflect underlying operational performance but rather the timing of non-cash or non-recurring charges. A period with high restructuring charges (e.g., $487 million in 2025) will show a much larger boost to Adjusted EBITDA than a period with minimal charges.

**In summary:** Management uses these measures for operational insight and benchmarking but explicitly warns they are non-standard, should not replace GAAP, and that their calculations may not be comparable to peers. The very nature of adding back variable, non-cash expenses like SBC and one-time charges like impairments inherently risks distorting performance comparisons both between the company's own segments and across different reporting periods.

Based on the provided text, here are the details for **Segment Adjusted EBITDA**, including its definition, components, cautionary language, and the full-year reconciliations for 2025 and 2024.

### **1. Definition & Components**

*   **Definition:** Segment Adjusted EBITDA is defined as **segment income (loss) from operations** excluding the following four items:
    1.  **Depreciation and amortization**
    2.  **Share-based compensation**
    3.  **Restructuring charges**
    4.  **Impairment**

### **2. Management's Use and Cautionary Language**

*   **Management's Use:** Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA are described as **key performance measures** that management uses to assess financial performance and for internal planning and forecasting.
*   **Usefulness for Investors:** Management considers them to be **meaningful performance measures** for investors to evaluate operating performance and compare financial results across periods.
*   **Cautionary Limitations:**
    *   The measures are **non-GAAP supplemental measures**.
    *   Other companies may calculate them differently or not at all, limiting their usefulness as comparative measures.
    *   Because of these limitations, they **should not be considered in isolation or as a substitute** for net income (loss), income (loss) from operations, or any other GAAP measure.
    *   They **should be considered together with GAAP financial measures** and the reconciliations provided.

### **3. Reconciliation for Full Year 2025 and 2024**

The following tables reconcile **Segment Income (loss) from operations** (the GAAP measure) to **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** (the non-GAAP measure) for each reportable segment.

**Year Ended December 31, 2025 ($ in millions)**
| Segment | Income (loss) from operations | Add: Depreciation & Amortization | Add: Share-based Compensation | Add: Restructuring Charges | Add: Impairment | **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Space** | $(657) | $757 | $515 | $- | $38 | **$653** |
| **Connectivity** | $4,423 | $2,376 | $369 | $- | $- | **$7,168** |
| **AI** | $(6,355) | $3,568 | $1,063 | $487 | $- | **$(1,237)** |
| **Total Reportable Segments** | **$(2,589)** | **$6,701** | **$1,947** | **$487** | **$38** | **$6,584** |

**Year Ended December 31, 2024 ($ in millions)**
| Segment | Income (loss) from operations | Add: Depreciation & Amortization | Add: Share-based Compensation | Add: Restructuring Charges | Add: Impairment | **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Space** | $21 | $637 | $472 | $- | $24 | **$1,154** |
| **Connectivity** | $2,006 | $1,508 | $296 | $- | $39 | **$3,849** |
| **AI** | $(1,561) | $1,679 | $16 | $213 | $- | **$347** |
| **Total Reportable Segments** | **$466** | **$3,824** | **$784** | **$213** | **$63** | **$5,350** |

arguments.final_result:
## Segment Adjusted EBITDA: Construction and Why It Matters for Segment Comparisons

### Definition

The SpaceX S-1 defines **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** as a segment's GAAP **income (loss) from operations**, adjusted by adding back four specific line items. It is the filing's primary non-GAAP segment performance measure, used by management for internal planning, forecasting, and for investors to evaluate operating performance across periods.

---

### The Four Add-Backs (from Segment Operating Income)

| # | Add-Back | Nature |
|---|----------|--------|
| 1 | **Depreciation and amortization** | Non-cash; allocation of tangible and intangible asset costs over useful lives |
| 2 | **Share-based compensation (SBC)** | Non-cash; expense related to stock-based awards granted to employees |
| 3 | **Restructuring charges** | Typically non-recurring; severance, facility closures, organizational reorganizations |
| 4 | **Impairment** | Non-cash write-down of long-lived assets when recoverable value falls below carrying amount |

The formula is:

> **Segment Adjusted EBITDA = Segment Income (Loss) from Operations + D&A + SBC + Restructuring + Impairment**

---

### Illustrative Reconciliation — FY 2025 (in millions)

| | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $(657) | $4,423 | $(6,355) | $(2,589) |
| Depreciation & amortization | 757 | 2,376 | 3,568 | 6,701 |
| Share-based compensation | 515 | 369 | 1,063 | 1,947 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | 487 | 487 |
| Impairment | 38 | — | — | 38 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$653** | **$7,168** | **$(1,237)** | **$6,584** |

The same GAAP operating loss of $(2,589) million is transformed into an Adjusted EBITDA of $6,584 million — a swing of $9,173 million, driven overwhelmingly by D&A ($6,701M) and SBC ($1,947M).

---

### Why Understanding Construction Matters Before Comparing Segment Economics

**1. The add-backs can reverse the profitability ranking of segments.**
In FY 2025, the **AI segment** had a GAAP operating loss of $(6,355)M but a Segment Adjusted EBITDA of only $(1,237)M — a $5,118M improvement driven by $3,568M of D&A and $1,063M of SBC add-backs. Without understanding this, an analyst would overstate AI's operational viability relative to its actual cash burn trajectory.

**2. Share-based compensation distorts inter-segment comparability.**
SBC is allocated unevenly across segments. In Q1 2026, the AI segment's SBC add-back was $378M versus $145M for Space and $116M for Connectivity. Because SBC represents real economic cost to shareholders (dilution), removing it inflates the apparent profitability of whichever segment grants more equity compensation. A segment with heavy SBC will appear to have better Adjusted EBITDA margins than one paying in cash, even if both have identical economic cost structures.

**3. Depreciation & amortization masks asset intensity differences.**
D&A was the largest single add-back in every period (e.g., $6,701M in FY 2025). The AI segment alone had $3,568M of D&A, reflecting massive capital expenditures on compute infrastructure. Removing D&A makes AI and Connectivity look more comparable on an Adjusted EBITDA basis, but the underlying asset base and required reinvestment differ enormously. Ignoring D&A can lead to overstating free cash flow generation capacity for capital-intensive segments.

**4. Restructuring and impairment charges are lumpy and non-comparable across periods.**
The AI segment recorded $487M of restructuring charges in FY 2025 (essentially all corporate restructuring), while Space had $38M of impairment. In Q1 2026, the AI segment actually recorded a *negative* restructuring add-back of $(11)M (a reversal). These items create period-over-period volatility that has nothing to do with recurring operational performance. Excluding them smooths earnings but can obscure real costs of strategic missteps or asset impairments.

**5. The filing's own cautionary language underscores non-comparability.**
The S-1 explicitly warns that "other companies in our industry may calculate Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA differently than we do or may not calculate them at all, limiting their usefulness as comparative measures." This means cross-company peer benchmarking using Adjusted EBITDA is inherently unreliable without normalizing for differing add-back definitions.

**6. Common-control accounting compounds the issue.**
The filing's financial statements have been retrospectively recast to include xAI and X Holdings for all periods. This means the SBC, D&A, restructuring, and impairment figures for each segment reflect the consolidated post-merger entity, not standalone SpaceX. Historical trends in the add-backs may reflect consolidation effects rather than organic changes in segment economics.

---

### Summary

Segment Adjusted EBITDA is a useful management tool for viewing cash-generative capacity before capital allocation decisions, but it is constructed by stripping out **$9.2 billion** of real costs in FY 2025 alone. Before comparing segment-to-segment economics — or comparing SpaceX's segments to peers — an analyst must understand:

- **Which add-backs dominate** (D&A is ~73% of total add-backs in FY 2025)
- **How unevenly they are distributed** across segments
- **Whether they represent truly non-recurring items** (restructuring/impairment) or **recurring economic costs** (SBC, D&A)
- **That the filing itself disclaims cross-company comparability** of this measure

Without this decomposition, Segment Adjusted EBITDA can materially misrepresent the relative profitability and capital needs of each business line.

---

### Sources

| Source | Name |
|--------|------|
| https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm | SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026) — MD&A Non-GAAP Reconciliation Tables and Segment Footnote |

results.content:
## Segment Adjusted EBITDA: Construction and Why It Matters for Segment Comparisons

### Definition

The SpaceX S-1 defines **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** as a segment's GAAP **income (loss) from operations**, adjusted by adding back four specific line items. It is the filing's primary non-GAAP segment performance measure, used by management for internal planning, forecasting, and for investors to evaluate operating performance across periods.

---

### The Four Add-Backs (from Segment Operating Income)

| # | Add-Back | Nature |
|---|----------|--------|
| 1 | **Depreciation and amortization** | Non-cash; allocation of tangible and intangible asset costs over useful lives |
| 2 | **Share-based compensation (SBC)** | Non-cash; expense related to stock-based awards granted to employees |
| 3 | **Restructuring charges** | Typically non-recurring; severance, facility closures, organizational reorganizations |
| 4 | **Impairment** | Non-cash write-down of long-lived assets when recoverable value falls below carrying amount |

The formula is:

> **Segment Adjusted EBITDA = Segment Income (Loss) from Operations + D&A + SBC + Restructuring + Impairment**

---

### Illustrative Reconciliation — FY 2025 (in millions)

| | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $(657) | $4,423 | $(6,355) | $(2,589) |
| Depreciation & amortization | 757 | 2,376 | 3,568 | 6,701 |
| Share-based compensation | 515 | 369 | 1,063 | 1,947 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | 487 | 487 |
| Impairment | 38 | — | — | 38 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$653** | **$7,168** | **$(1,237)** | **$6,584** |

The same GAAP operating loss of $(2,589) million is transformed into an Adjusted EBITDA of $6,584 million — a swing of $9,173 million, driven overwhelmingly by D&A ($6,701M) and SBC ($1,947M).

---

### Why Understanding Construction Matters Before Comparing Segment Economics

**1. The add-backs can reverse the profitability ranking of segments.**
In FY 2025, the **AI segment** had a GAAP operating loss of $(6,355)M but a Segment Adjusted EBITDA of only $(1,237)M — a $5,118M improvement driven by $3,568M of D&A and $1,063M of SBC add-backs. Without understanding this, an analyst would overstate AI's operational viability relative to its actual cash burn trajectory.

**2. Share-based compensation distorts inter-segment comparability.**
SBC is allocated unevenly across segments. In Q1 2026, the AI segment's SBC add-back was $378M versus $145M for Space and $116M for Connectivity. Because SBC represents real economic cost to shareholders (dilution), removing it inflates the apparent profitability of whichever segment grants more equity compensation. A segment with heavy SBC will appear to have better Adjusted EBITDA margins than one paying in cash, even if both have identical economic cost structures.

**3. Depreciation & amortization masks asset intensity differences.**
D&A was the largest single add-back in every period (e.g., $6,701M in FY 2025). The AI segment alone had $3,568M of D&A, reflecting massive capital expenditures on compute infrastructure. Removing D&A makes AI and Connectivity look more comparable on an Adjusted EBITDA basis, but the underlying asset base and required reinvestment differ enormously. Ignoring D&A can lead to overstating free cash flow generation capacity for capital-intensive segments.

**4. Restructuring and impairment charges are lumpy and non-comparable across periods.**
The AI segment recorded $487M of restructuring charges in FY 2025 (essentially all corporate restructuring), while Space had $38M of impairment. In Q1 2026, the AI segment actually recorded a *negative* restructuring add-back of $(11)M (a reversal). These items create period-over-period volatility that has nothing to do with recurring operational performance. Excluding them smooths earnings but can obscure real costs of strategic missteps or asset impairments.

**5. The filing's own cautionary language underscores non-comparability.**
The S-1 explicitly warns that "other companies in our industry may calculate Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA differently than we do or may not calculate them at all, limiting their usefulness as comparative measures." This means cross-company peer benchmarking using Adjusted EBITDA is inherently unreliable without normalizing for differing add-back definitions.

**6. Common-control accounting compounds the issue.**
The filing's financial statements have been retrospectively recast to include xAI and X Holdings for all periods. This means the SBC, D&A, restructuring, and impairment figures for each segment reflect the consolidated post-merger entity, not standalone SpaceX. Historical trends in the add-backs may reflect consolidation effects rather than organic changes in segment economics.

---

### Summary

Segment Adjusted EBITDA is a useful management tool for viewing cash-generative capacity before capital allocation decisions, but it is constructed by stripping out **$9.2 billion** of real costs in FY 2025 alone. Before comparing segment-to-segment economics — or comparing SpaceX's segments to peers — an analyst must understand:

- **Which add-backs dominate** (D&A is ~73% of total add-backs in FY 2025)
- **How unevenly they are distributed** across segments
- **Whether they represent truly non-recurring items** (restructuring/impairment) or **recurring economic costs** (SBC, D&A)
- **That the filing itself disclaims cross-company comparability** of this measure

Without this decomposition, Segment Adjusted EBITDA can materially misrepresent the relative profitability and capital needs of each business line.

---

| Source | Name |
